State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322.5660          322.5660         322.5901         322.6441          322.6444

June 13, 1983

Joseph A. Forest
City Attorney
City of Novato
P.O. Box 205
Novato, CA 94948

Re: Your Request for Written
Advice
Our No. A-83-126

Dear Mr. Forest:

Your letter requests written advice pursuant to Government
Code Section 83114(b).1/ You state the facts as follows:

FACTS

Government Code Section 65852.2 provides that cities may by
ordinance provide for the creation of second units in single-
family and multifamily residential zones consistent with certain
restrictions. Unless a city adopts its ordinance in this regard
by July 1, 1983, a city must process applications for second
units pursuant to the requirements listed in Section 65852.2.

The City of Novato has chosen to implement Section 65852.2
by adopting an ordinance. A proposed ordinance permitting
second units in single-family zones was considered by the Novato
City Council at its April 19, 1983 meeting, and received a (2-2)
vote. The fifth member of the Council, Margaret Kapranos, was
absent from the meeting due to the death of a family member. In
light of the tie vote, the ordinance was to be reconsidered at a
future meeting when Ms. Kapranos would be present. Subsequent
to the April 19, 1983 meeting, you were advised by the Novato

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code,
unless otherwise stated.

Joseph A. Forest
June 13, 1983
Page 2


City Manager tnat Mrs. Kapranos' nusband, David Kapranos, is a
general contractor in Novato. The business is community
property. The business is small to medium sized, and would be
quite capable of contracting for second unit additions. There
is concern that Mrs. Kapranos may have a conflict of interest,
based upon her financial interest in their general contracting
business under Section 87100, which would prevent her from
voting on the proposed ordinance. She has asked that you seek
our advice on this question.

QUESTION

Your specific question is stated as follows:

Under Section 87103, would Mrs. Kapranos' decision
on the proposed second unit ordinance have a material
financial effect on their business which is distin­
guishable from its effect on the public generally?

ANALYSIS

From the facts stated in your letter it is clear that
Mrs. Kapranos is a public official who has an investment of more
than $1,000 in her husband's business which is also a source of
income to her of $250 or more during the last 12 months under
Section 87103(a) and (c). Therefore, the question is narrowed
to whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on the business distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally.

Pursuant to Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18703, it is clear that the effect, if any, on Mr. Kapranos'
construction business will be distinguishable from the effect
upon the public generally.

We turn now to the remaining question, that of the
reasonably foreseeable financial effect and whether or not it
will be material. As indicated in your letter, Section 65852.2
provides that if a city fails to adopt an ordinance by July 1, 1983, the provisions of Section
65852.2(b) will take effect and permit the construction of
second units in Novato under certain conditions. Consequently,
the effect which must be examined is that resulting from any
differences between the Novato ordinance in question and the
provisions of Section 65852.2(b).

Joseph A. Forest
June 13, 1983
Page 3


     Having now received a copy of the Novato Ordinance, it is
clear that certain differences are present.  What is much less
clear is their practical effect upon Mr. Kapranos' business.

     Essentially, the Novato Ordinance (copy attached) is more
permissive than Section 65852.2(b) in permitting construction of
detached accessory dwellings; however, the ordinance is less
permissive in that it permits accessory dwellings only where the
original dwelling or the accessory dwelling (once completed)
will be occupied as the principal residence of the owner of the
lot in question.  Consequently, it is exceedingly difficult for
us to determine whether adoption of the Novato Ordinance (rather
than no ordinance, in which case Section 65852.2(b) becomes
operative) will reasonably and foreseeably have a material
financial effect upon Mr. Kapranos' construction business.  If
the options were the ordinance versus no accessory dwellings at
all, the task would be much easier.  But that is not what we
understand to be the circumstances in this instance.

     Under the standards set forth in the Commission's Thorner
Opinion, No.75-089, 1 FPPC Opinions 198, Dec. 4, 1975, in order
for an effect to be reasonably foreseeable it must be more than
a mere possibility; there must be a substantial probability.
(1 FPPC Opinions at 203-206.)  We have no basis, on the facts
presented to us, to conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable
that Mrs. Kapranos would have a conflict of interest based upon
her financial interest in her husband's business which would
require disqualification as to this ordinance.  However, if you
are aware of facts which indicate that either alternative would
result in an increase in Mr. Kapranos' business, then please
advise us.  Otherwise, she may participate in consideration of
the ordinance.

     Should you have further questions, please do not hesitate to
call me at (916) 322-5901.

                              Sincerely,


                              Robert E. Leigigh
                              Counsel
                              Legal Division

REL:pln
Enclosure

Joseph A. Forest
A PROFESSIONAL LAW CORPORATION
P.O. BOX 205
NOVATO, CA 94948

(415) 898.1441

May 2, 1983


State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Commission Members:

I am the City Attorney for the City of Novato. I have recently been
called upon to render an opinion concerning whether a member of our City
Council has a conflict of interest which prevents her from voting on a proposed
ordinance. As permitted under Government Code Section 83114 (b), I am
requesting your written opinion in this matter. The pertinent facts are as
follows.

Government Code Section 65852.2 provides that cities may by ordinance
provide for the creation of second units in single-family and multifamily
residential zones consistent with certain restrictions. Unless a city adopts its
ordinance in this regard by July 1, 1983, then a city must process applications
for second units pursuant to the requirements listed in Section 65852.2.

The City of Novato has chosen to implement Section 65852.2 by attempting
to adopt an ordinance. A proposed ordinance permitting second units in single-
family zones was considered by the Novato City Council at its April 19, 1983
meeting, and received a (2-2) vote. The fifth member of the Council, Margaret
Kapranos, was absent from the meeting due to the death of a family member.
In light of the tie vote, the ordinance was to be reconsidered at a future
meeting when Mrs. Kapranos would be present. Subsequent to the April 19,
1983 meeting, this office was advised by the Novato City Manager that Mrs.
Kapranos' husband, David Kapranos, is a general contractor in Novato. The
business is community property. The business is small to medium sized, and
would be quite capable of contracting for second unit additions. There is
concern that Mrs. Kapranos may have a conflict of interest, based upon her
financial interest in their general contracting business under Section 87100,
which would prevent her from voting on the proposed ordinance.

Fair Political Practices Commission
May 2, 1983
Page two

     Specifically, my question is as follows:

          Under Section 87103, would Mrs. Kapranos' decision
          on the proposed second unit ordinance have a material
          financial effect on their business which is
          distinguishable from its effect on the public
          generally?

     Your assistance in this matter will be greatly appreciated.

                                                Very truly yours,

                                                Joseph A. Forest

JAF:jf

cc:   Mr. Phil Brown
      City Manager
      City of Novato
      901 Sherman Avenue
      Novato, CA 94947

CITY OF NOVATO

ORDINANCE NO. _______

AN ORDINANCE OF THE CITY OF NOVATO AMENDING CHAPTER XIX,
ZONING, OF THE NOVATO MUNICIPAL CODE RELATING TO ACCESSORY
DWELLINGS

THE CITY OF NOVATO CITY COUNCIL DOES HEREBY ORDAIN AS FOLLOWS:

SECTION 1. Section 19-10.004 of the Novato Municipal Code shall be amended
to include the following definition:

"Accessory Dwelling" shall mean a permanently constructed, additional dwelling
that is entirely contained within, attached by a common wall and roof to, or
erected separate from an existing residence on a single-family lot, use of
which is not intended for sale and may be rented. It shall be built as a com-
pletely self-contained and independently heated domicile for one household,
with its own permanent installed facilities for cooking, sanitation and
hygiene. A temporary building or a vehicle shall not qualify as an accessory
dwelling.

SECTION 2. A new Section 19-24 is hereby added to the Novato Municipal
Code as follows:

19-24 Accessory Dwellings

19-24.002 General Provisions

a. Purpose. This section is adopted to carry out Novato's General Plan hous-
ing policy regarding secondary residences and to provide for the orderly
development of accessory dwellings on certain residentially zoned lots,
pursuant to Chapter 1440 of the California Statutes of 1982 (Government
Code Section 65852.1 et seq.). The purpose of this ordinance is to allow
the creation of accessory dwellings through the voluntary action of indi-
vidual homeowners, thus providing an opportunity for partially achieving
the City's rental housing objectives without reliance on public subsidy
or major new construction. As a response to changing family lifestyles
and changing social values regarding the role housing should play in the
community, this program for accessory dwellings is established as a means
by which diversity can be achieved and shared throughout the entire city
and as a method to promote variety and choice within Novato's housing
sector. To encourage and facilitate owner-occupant's ability to financial-
ly maintain homeownership and occupancy in the neighborhood, and in recog-
nition of the authority provided by California Government Code Section
65852.2, it is necessary to limit the construction of accessory dwellings
to owner-occupants.

b. Applicability

1. Locations: Accessory dwellings shall be permitted in A-2 (Limited
Agricultural) Districts, R-1 (One-Family Residence) Districts, and
R-SP (Planned Single-Family Detached) Districts. Accessory dwellings
shall not encroach or be placed upon privately owned open space or
scenic easements. This ordinance is not applicable for multi-family
zoning districts, except that properties containing an existing single-
family dwelling which are situated in multi-family zoning districts
may be allowed to establish an additional dwelling under the provisions
of this zoning ordinance.

2. Owner-occupancy: The construction or use of accessory dwellings is
limited to owner-occupants. The owner of a lot proposed for accessory
dwelling use must occupy as principal residence either the primary
dwelling or the accessory dwelling.

3. One accessory dwelling per lot: In addition to an existing dwelling,
one accessory dwelling may be constructed on a legally developable
lot. Subject to the approval of the Zoning Administrator, an exist-
ing dwelling may be designated as an accessory dwelling to allow
construction of a new principal dwelling on a legally developable
lot.

H1/H(1)
5/26/83

4.    Separate ownership prohibited: No subdivision of property shall be
allowed which will create a separate ownership of the accessory dwell-
ing or a separate ownership of property within the existing residen-
tial lot.

5.    Illegally built units: Accessory dwellings built prior to the adop-
tion of this ordinance may be legalized by applying for a use permit.
A use permit may be granted for an existing, illegally built accessory
dwelling provided that a) the Zoning Administrator determines the
accessory dwelling substantially complies with the provisions of
this section; and b) any deficiency of the unit affecting the health
or safety of occupants is corrected in accordance with State and
local regulations.

C.    Establishment

1.    Use permit required: A use permit shall be required for an accessory
dwelling, subject to the provisions of this section and Section 19-20
of this Chapter relating to Use Permits.

2.    Expiration: Once the accessory dwelling is constructed, the rights
under the use permit shall run with the land, unless the Zoning Admin-
istrator imposes a specific condition for expiration based on unique
or unusual circumstances or when the use permit automatically becomes
void pursuant to Section 19-20.010 of this Chapter.

3.    Use permit recorded: A document showing approval of the use permit,
containing a description of the property, and listing any conditions
of approval, shall be recorded by the City of Novato with the official
records of Marin County. This shall be done prior to issuing an
Occupancy Permit for the accessory dwelling.

4.    Revocation of a use permit: Pursuant to Section 19-1.016 of this
Chapter relating to Enforcement, the Zoning Administrator may ini-
tiate proceedings to revoke a use permit for an accessory dwelling
in accordance with Section 19-20.012 of this Chapter. A notice of
revocation, documenting the cause for revocation and the action of
the City Council, shall be recorded by the City of Novato with the
official records of Marin County.

5.    Rescinding a use permit: The owner of record for property containing
an accessory dwelling may request that the use permit be rescinded.
Requests shall be made in writing to the Zoning Administrator and
must be accompanied by evidence that the accessory dwelling has been
dismantled, or removal of the accessory dwelling substantiated by an
on-premise visit to the property by the Zoning Administrator. A
notice documenting the rescission of a use permit by the Zoning Admin-
istrator shall be recorded by the City of Novato with the official
records of Marin County. Subsequent reinstatement of an accessory
dwelling on the property shall require the issuance of a new use
permit pursuant to the provisions of this section.

d.    Applications and Processing

1.    Application: Application for an accessory dwelling must be made by
owners of the property upon which the accessory dwelling is proposed,
and shall include a certification that the owner resides on the prop-
erty, or will reside there upon completion of an accessory dwelling.

2.    How to apply: Applications for an accessory dwelling shall be made
to the Zoning Administrator in the form required by the Department
of Community Development. Each application must be accompanied by
the required fee(s) and such information necessary to determine eli-
gibility and conformance to the provisions of this section.

3.    Content of applications: Applicants for an accessory dwelling shall
submit the following:

a.    The name and address of the owner(s) of record and their signa-
tures;


H1/M(2)
5/26/83

b.     The address of the established residence;

c.     The Assessor's Parcel Number;

d.     The heated floor area of the principal, main dwelling;

e.     The floor area of the proposed accessory dwelling; the floor
       area of the existing dwelling to be included in or reconstructed
       to the floor area of the accessory dwelling;

f.     A map (can be freehand) showing the location of the property in
       relation to nearby major streets.

g.     A plot plan at a minimum scale of 1" = 20' with dimensions of
       wall lines and distances from structures to nearest property
       line (measured perpendicular from property line). The plot
       plan must show all property lines, the location of the existing
       dwelling, the location of accessory structures, the proposed
       location of the accessory dwelling, the location of all present
       and proposed vehicular parking and driveways, the location of
       all utility connections and laterals serving the lot, the loca-
       tion of all private or public easements encumbering the property,
       and the location of existing frontage improvements (curbs,
       gutters, sidewalks, edge of paving);

h.     Site plans and drawings showing the accessory dwelling dimen-
       sions in plan view and elevation view in relationship to the
       main dwelling. A description of the materials, landscaping and
       color treatments to be used should be indicated on these draw-
       ings;

i.     Written consent from the owner of record to a physical inspec-
       tion of the premises prior to the issuance of a Use Permit; and

j.     A utility sign-off form, to be provided by the Department of
       Community Development, which will indicate whether or not water,
       sewer, telephone, cable, fire protection, police, and energy
       service capacity is available and adequate to serve the existing
       dwelling and accessory dwelling.

k.     Such other information as the Zoning Administrator may need to
       establish whether or not the proposal conforms to this ordinance.

4.     Noticed public hearing required: The public hearing shall be noticed
       in accordance with Subsection 19-5.0l4b1(b)(5) of this Chapter relat-
       ing to Public Hearings Requiring Notification: Use Permits.

5.     Design review: The Zoning Administrator may refer the plans for an
       accessory dwelling to the Design Review Committee for comment at
       their next available public hearing.

e.     Findings: In addition to the findings required by Section 19-20.006 of
       this Chapter, the Zoning Administrator shall also make the following find-
       ings in order to approve a use permit for an accessory dwelling:

       1.     That the decision to approve an accessory dwelling is consistent
              with the goals, policies and objectives set forth in the Novato Gen-
              eral Plan;

       2.     That the accessory dwelling conforms to the regulations and standards
              set forth in this section and that of the zoning district in which
              it is located, and that it will conform to Novato's Uniform Building/
              Housing Code and Uniform Fire Code;

       3.     That public and utility service levels are adequate to serve both
              dwellings.

       4.     That the design of the accessory dwelling conforms to the provisions
              of the ordinance for accessory dwellings and is compatible with the
              design of the existing dwelling (uses substantially the same colors,
              materials, and design on the exterior) and the general character of
              neighboring residential properties;

#1/M(1)
5/26/83

5.    That the owner of record agrees to maintain their principal residence
on the property.

19-24.004  Development Regulations and Standards for Accessory Dwellings

a.    Conformance to zoning district regulations: Unless otherwise specified
in this section, all regulations of the zoning district within which the
lot is located shall prevail and be applicable to the accessory dwelling
in the same manner as the principal dwelling. This includes compliance
with master plan and precise development plan conditions of approval for
accessory dwellings to be located in RSP districts. Accessory dwellings
to be located in A-2 and R-1 districts shall observe standards for building site area, yard areas and setbacks, lot coverage, floor areas, and
height.

b.    Variances from any physical standards may be applied for in accordance
with Section 19-19 of this ordinance. However, with respect to any variance from existing standards for accessory dwellings, the Zoning Administrator shall take into consideration the location of the property with
respect to bus routes and pedestrian travel to necessary facilities and
services.

c.    Parking: Notwithstanding Subsection 19-16.008b1 of this Chapter, relating
to Number of Parking Spaces Required, a minimum of 3 off-street parking
spaces shall be required for a lot containing an accessory dwelling. One
parking space shall be covered and one parking space shall serve the accessory dwelling.

d.    Utilities and Services: Utility and service agencies shall be given the
opportunity to review and comment on the proposed plans for an accessory
dwelling before the use permit public hearing date. Notwithstanding Section 5-53 of the Development Standards Ordinance, undergrounding of utility
service to the accessory dwelling shall only be required for lots where
underground utility lines already serve the property.

e.    Septic systems: For lots outside the service boundary of the Novato Sanitary District, the City Engineer shall review and approve of the adequacy
of a private septic system to serve the sanitary needs of both dwellings
in accordance with Section 5-37 of the Development Standards Ordinance.

19-24.006  Design Regulations and Standards for Accessory Dwellings

a.    Accessory dwellings shall be constructed in conformance with all provisions
of the Novato Municipal Code and other applicable laws, including but not
limited to Chapter IV (Building and Housing), Chapter V (Development Standards), Chapter XIX (Zoning), except where deviations or exceptions are
specified in this section.

b.    Exterior surfaces and dimensions of the accessory dwelling shall be reasonably integrated with the design of the existing dwelling and compatible
with the general character of neighboring residential properties. Accessory dwellings shall use colors, materials and design on the building
exterior which are substantially the same as those on the primary dwelling.

c.    Alphanumeric street numbers shall be assigned to all accessory dwellings
having a separate, exterior entry. The street number is to consist of
the existing dwelling's number followed by "-AD" to readily distinguish
an accessory dwelling's address for emergency dispatch reasons. Mail
drops for accessory dwellings should be provided in the front of the lot,
except in situations where the accessory dwelling takes access from another
street or an alley.

d.    Contained designs: A type of accessory dwelling which is constructed
entirely within the existing dwelling or garage or which has its floor
area converted from the existing dwelling.

In addition to the general design standards and regulations, contained
accessory dwellings shall observe the following design standards:

1.    Maximum accessory dwelling size: 35% of existing heated floor area,
not to exceed 550 square feet.


H1/M(4)
5/26/83

2.     Minimum accessory dwelling size: 220 square feet.

3.     Heated floor area of the existing dwelling shall not be reduced by
       more than 35% to accommodate a contained design.

e.     Attached designs: A type of accessory dwelling in which common exterior
       structural elements of the existing dwelling, such as a roof and load
       bearing wall, are integrated into the design and/or extended, without
       separation, as an addition to the existing dwelling.

       In addition to the general design regulations and standards, attached
       accessory dwellings shall observe the following design standards:

       1.     Minimum lot size: 7500 square feet.

       2.     Maximum accessory dwelling size: 50% of existing heated floor area,
              not to exceed 750 square feet.

       3.     Minimum accessory dwelling size: 350 square feet.

       4.     Heated floor area of the existing dwelling shall not be reduced by
              more than 20% to accommodate an attached design.

       5.     A separate exterior entry shall be provided to serve the accessory
              dwelling. Interior doorways between the existing dwelling and the
              attached accessory dwelling should be avoided.

f.     Detached designs: A type of accessory dwelling which is structurally
       independent and separated from the existing dwelling.

       In addition to the general design regulations and standards, detached
       accessory dwellings shall observe the following design standards:

       1.     Minimum lot size: 10,000 square feet.

       2.     Maximum accessory dwelling size: 60% of existing heated floor area,
              not to exceed 1,000 square feet.

       3.     Minimum accessory dwelling size: 350 square feet.

       4.     A minimum setback of 8 feet between the exterior walls of the exist-
              ing dwelling and the accessory dwelling shall be observed in detached
              designs.

       SECTION 3.     Subsection "a" of Section 19-11.004 of the Novato Municipal
Code is hereby amended as follows:

       6.     Accessory dwellings, accessory buildings and accessory uses.

       SECTION 4.     Subsection "a" of Section 19-11.012 of the Novato Municipal
Code is hereby amended as follows:

       7.     Accessory dwellings, accessory buildings and accessory uses.

       SECTION 5.     Subsection "d.2" of Section 19-11.020 of the Novato Municip-
al Code is hereby amended as follows:

       (c)   Accessory dwellings.

       SECTION 6.     Subsection "c" of Section 19-14.002 of the Novato Municipal
Code is hereby amended as follows:

       7.     To permit the placement of a permanently constructed accessory dwel-
              ling on a residential lot zoned for single-family use in accordance
              with Section 19-24 of this chapter.

       SECTION 7.     Section 19-21.002 of the Novato Municipal Code is hereby
amended as follows:

       b.     Applicability. A mobile home may be placed on a lot, or subdivision,
              zoned for single-family use upon approval of a mobile home designa-
              tion or, in the case of its use as an accessory dwelling, approval

              HJ/HM(5)
              5/26/83

of a use permit in accordance with Section 19-24 of this chapter.
All mobile home additions, constructed according to Federal Standards,
will require an amended designation approved according to the same
procedure and criteria established for base mobile homes. Mobile
home additions shall not be permitted to be attached to convention-
ally-built homes.

SECTION 8. Subsection "f" of Section 19-21.002 of the Novato Municipal
Code is hereby amended as follows:

2. The mobile home is to be used as a single-family dwelling or as an
accessory dwelling.

3. The proposal conforms to all zoning, subdivision and development
standards to which a conventional single-family residential dwelling
on the same lot would be subject. In the case of its use as an acces-
sory dwelling, the mobile home must conform to the provisions con-
tained within Section 19-24 of this chapter.

SECTION 9. Subsection "a" of Section 19-21.002 of the Novato Municipal
Code is hereby amended as follows:

1. The mobile home is a double-wide or larger multi-sectional unit when
it is to be used as the principal, main dwelling on the lot. A mobile
home used as the principal, main dwelling must be a minimum of 20
feet wide. A mobile home used as an accessory dwelling may be less
than 20 feet wide.

SECTION 10. Severability: The City Council hereby declares that it would
have passed this ordinance sentence by sentence, paragraph by paragraph, and
section by section. If for any reason any sentence, paragraph, or section of
this ordinance shall be held invalid or unconstitutional, such decision shall
not affect the validity of the remaining parts of this ordinance.

SECTION 11. Posting and Effective Date: This ordinance shall be published
once in the Novato Advance, a newspaper of general circulation published in
the City of Novato, within fifteen (15) days after its passage and adoption.
The effective date of this ordinance shall be the 30th day after the day of
its passage and adoption by the City Council.

* * * * *

THE FOREGOING ORDINANCE was first read at a regular meeting of the Novato City
Council on the __ day of __________, 1983, and was passed and adopted at a
regular meeting of the Novato City Council on the __ day of ______________,
1983, by the following vote:

AYES: Council Members

NOES: Council Members

ABSENT: Council Members


_________________________________
Mayor of the City of Novato

Approved as to form:


_________________________________  _________________________________
Joseph A. Forest, City Attorney     Attest:


                                    _________________________________
                                    City Clerk of the City of Novato


HI/M(6)
5/26/83

lot in question. 
Consequently, it is exceedingly difficult for 
us to determine whether adoption of the Novato Ordinance (rather 
than no ordinance, in wnich case Sectlon 65852.2(0) becomes 
operative) will reasonably and foreseeably have a material 
financial effect upon Mr. Kapranos' construction business. 
If 
the options were the ordinance versus no accessory dwellings at 
all, the task woula be much easier. 
But that is not what we 
unaerstand to be the circumstances in this instance. 
Under the stanaards set forth in the Commission's Thorner 
Opinion, No.75-089, 1 FPPC Opinions 198, Dec. 4, 1975, in order 
for an effect to be reasonably foreseeable It must De more 
~nan 
a mere possibllity; there must be a sUbstantial probability. 
(1 FPPC Opinions at 203-206.) 
We have no basis, on the facts 
presented to us, to conclude that It is reasonably foreseeable 
that Mrs. Kapranos would have a confllct of interest based upon 
her financlal lnterest in her husband's business which woula 
require aisqualification as to this ordinance. 
However, if you 
are aware of facts which indicate that either alternative would 
result in an increase in Mr. Kapranos' buslness, then please 
advise us. 
Otherwise, she may participate in consideration of 
the ordinance.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city council member who has a financial interest in her husband's general contracting business would have a material conflict of interest in voting on a proposed ordinance permitting second unit construction, given that the ordinance differs from the default statutory provisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC found no reasonable basis to conclude that Mrs. Kapranos has a material conflict of interest requiring disqualification, as it is not substantially foreseeable that the ordinance (versus the default statutory provisions) would have a materially different financial effect on the husband's construction business. She may vote unless facts show either alternative would demonstrably increase his business.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Complete advice letter with full question and conclusion sections. Includes incoming request letter from City Attorney followed by FPPC response with detailed analysis. Attachments include proposed Novato ordinance. No withdrawal or declination elements present.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your specific question is statea as follows: 
Under Section 87103, would Mrs. Kapranos' decIsion 
on the proposed secona unit orainance have a material 
financial effect on their business whiCh is distin-
guishable from its effect on the PUbliC generally?

CONCLUSION: We have no basis, on the facts presented to us, to conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable that Mrs. Kapranos would have a conflict of interest based upon her financial interest in her husband's business which would require disqualification as to this ordinance. However, if you are aware of facts which indicate that either alternative would result in an increase in Mr. Kapranos' business, then please advise us. Otherwise, she may participate in consideration of the ordinance.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322.5660 322.5660 322.5901 322.6441 322.6444 June 13, 1983 Joseph A. Forest City Attorney City of Novato P.O. Box 205 Novato, CA 94948 Re: Your Request for Written Advice Our No. A-83-126 Dear Mr. Forest: Your letter requests written advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b).1/ You state the facts as follows: FACTS Government Code Section 65852.2 provides that cities may by ordinance provide for the creation of second units in single- family and multifamily residential zones consistent with certain restrictions. Unless a city adopts its ordinance in this regard by July 1, 1983, a city must process applications for second units pursuant to the requirements listed in Section 65852.2. The City of Novato has chosen to implement Section 65852.2 by adopting an ordinance. A proposed ordinance permitting second units in single-family zones was considered by the Novato City Council at its April 19, 1983 meeting, and received a (2-2) vote. The fifth member of the Council, Margaret Kapranos, was absent from the meeting due to the death of a family member. In light of the tie vote, the ordinance was to be reconsidered at a future meeting when Ms. Kapranos would be present. Subsequent to the April 19, 1983 meeting, you were advised by the Novato 1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise stated. Joseph A. Forest June 13, 1983 Page 2 City Manager tnat Mrs. Kapranos' nusband, David Kapranos, is a general contractor in Novato. The business is community property. The business is small to medium sized, and would be quite capable of contracting for second unit additions. There is concern that Mrs. Kapranos may have a conflict of interest, based upon her financial interest in their general contracting business under Section 87100, which would prevent her from voting on the proposed ordinance. She has asked that you seek our advice on this question. QUESTION Your specific question is stated as follows: Under Section 87103, would Mrs. Kapranos' decision on the proposed second unit ordinance have a material financial effect on their business which is distin­ guishable from its effect on the public generally? ANALYSIS From the facts stated in your letter it is clear that Mrs. Kapranos is a public official who has an investment of more than $1,000 in her husband's business which is also a source of income to her of $250 or more during the last 12 months under Section 87103(a) and (c). Therefore, the question is narrowed to whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the business distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. Pursuant to Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703, it is clear that the effect, if any, on Mr. Kapranos' construction business will be distinguishable from
embedding.summary: The City Attorney of Novato requested FPPC advice on whether a city council member with a financial interest in her husband's general contracting business has a conflict of interest in voting on a proposed accessory dwelling ordinance. The FPPC concluded no conflict exists because the practical effect of the ordinance versus the default statutory regime on the contractor's business is too speculative to meet the materiality standard.
